title: Dispatchschema bounded allocation
motivation: The JSON-Schema-lite parser shipped under the dispatch-structured-output PRD enforces no upper bound on per-field enum length, top-level required[] length, or top-level properties count, and the MCP schema-input boundary accepts unbounded byte payloads. A hostile or buggy client can submit a 50 MB schema with a million-entry enum and the parser will allocate it. Two safety-auditor runs and a unanimous pre-check roundtable flagged this; the prior tech-debt entry to add bounded-decode guards did not land before the schema parameter wiring shipped. This PRD closes that gap with a layered defense: byte cap at the MCP boundary, structural caps inside the parser, and typed errors on each reject path.

included: Bounded-allocation guards inside the JSON-Schema-lite parser for enum length, required[] length, and top-level properties count.
Byte-size cap on the MCP boundary's schema input ahead of parser invocation, mirroring the existing invalid-schema error path.
Typed bound-violation errors distinguishable from generic malformed-JSON errors.
Test coverage of each cap including exact-at-boundary cases.
excluded: Nested object recursion limits beyond the three structural caps.
Registering an INV-### entry in CLAUDE.md (no Safety Rules section exists yet; tracked separately as a cross-cutting chore).

design_facts.decision: Apply a byte-size cap at the MCP boundary AND structural caps inside the parser, not one or the other.
design_facts.rationale: Byte cap rejects pathological payloads before allocation; structural caps catch crafted-small-but-deep schemas that pass the byte cap.
Round numbers chosen as obviously enough room for any real schema while bounding worst-case allocation.

design_facts.decision: Reject paths return a typed structured error distinguishable from malformed JSON; choice of unified KindBoundExceeded vs per-cap kinds is implementer's call.
design_facts.rationale: Existing dispatchschema package already has a Kind-enum ParseError envelope; the test oracle asserts structure, not specific Kind names.

design_facts.decision: Parser caps and MCP byte cap ship as one cohesive feature touching two packages.
design_facts.rationale: They enforce a single layered defense; splitting would be artificial.

design_facts.decision: Do not cite or invent an invariant ID for this work.
design_facts.rationale: Repo currently has no registered invariants; adding a Safety Rules section is a separate cross-cutting chore.

features.title: Bounded-allocation guards in dispatchschema parser and MCP schema-input boundary

assertions: Calling `Parse` with a schema whose enum has 257 entries returns the typed bound-exceeded error; 256 entries passes.
Calling `Parse` with a schema whose top-level `required` has 257 entries returns the typed bound-exceeded error; 256 entries passes.
Calling `Parse` with a schema whose top-level `properties` count is 257 returns the typed bound-exceeded error; 256 entries passes.
Calling the MCP boundary with `input.Schema` of 65537 bytes returns the wrapped `invalid schema parameter:` error before Parse runs; 65536 bytes passes.
Bound-violation errors from `Parse` are structurally distinguishable from generic malformed-JSON errors (caller can branch on a typed Kind, not a string match).
Existing parser, prompt-suffix, validator, and five-tool MCP wiring tests continue to pass.
oracle.tooling: Go testing package; table-driven tests for each cap with exact-at-boundary cases (N=256 pass, N=257 fail; bytes=65536 pass, bytes=65537 fail).
